_description: Model derivations for Argentina vs Switzerland QF (2026-07-12). RULE1 applied throughout: Smarkets fetched first (02_smarkets_quotes_raw.json), before any estimate finalized. This match's Elo gap (+213.96) is almost identical in size to Spain-Belgium's (+216.61), the exact conditions that produced yesterday's SYMMETRIC_REGIME_COVERAGE failure -- treated as a high-alert case throughout.

monte_carlo_simulation.note: Unlike Norway-England, this match's goal-total model agrees well with market (see market_gap_log) -- the earlier lambda-underestimation issue was specific to that matchup's coefficients, not a universal model bug.

market_gap_log._description: Every case this session where |model - market| exceeded ~15pp.

entries.question: 4+ total cards
entries.recheck: Model's team-level own-card rates for both teams are low this tournament (Argentina 0.6/game, Switzerland 1.2/game), pulling the blended lambda down to 2.46. Market may be pricing in the tension/rivalry narrative around this specific fixture (high-stakes QF, 'controversial referee' storyline) that a pure historical-rate model can't see. Pinheiro's own small sample (n=2) includes a red card in one match.
entries.resolution: Weighted toward market given the exact-match line and the model's own admitted blind spot to fixture-specific tension. Final: 0.37.

entries.question: Julian Alvarez scores or assists
entries.recheck: Largest gap of the session. 0 goals AND 0 assists in all 5 games, AND his underlying shot volume is also minimal (5 shots total, never more than 2 in a game) -- this is corroborating evidence of low involvement, not just finishing variance. No historical StatsBomb rows exist for him to arbitrate (data-coverage gap). This matches the exact shape of the Yamal reputation-inflation pattern this project has validated three separate times this tournament (market prices a big-name attacker on reputation/role expectation, not current production).
entries.resolution: Priced well below market given the corroborated (not just outcome-based) evidence of low involvement, but not at the extreme empirical 0% given n=5 and RULE6 discipline. Final: 0.32.

entries.question: Switzerland 3+ shots on target
entries.recheck: This is the direct test case for SYMMETRIC_REGIME_COVERAGE -- see the dedicated section below. Even after re-weighting toward Switzerland's 2 toughest opponents (Algeria, Colombia: mean 3.5 SOT vs the full-5-game mean of 5.0), the model still runs 16pp above market.
entries.resolution: Weighted market more heavily than a simple average, given the model (even adjusted) shows a persistent tendency to overrate Switzerland's output and the market has visibility into team news/tactics this count-based model doesn't. Final: 0.62.

entries.question: Switzerland 4+ corners
entries.recheck: Same mechanism and same direction as the SOT case above -- larger residual gap even after adjustment.
entries.resolution: Weighted market more heavily, same logic as above. Final: 0.45.

symmetric_regime_coverage_check._description: This match has almost the exact Elo-gap magnitude (+213.96) that produced yesterday's Q12 failure on Belgium's SOT prop (+216.61). Treated as the primary test of whether the restored discipline actually works under the same conditions that broke it before.
symmetric_regime_coverage_check.switzerland_own_props_check: Switzerland's SOT and corners both show a clear, monotonic decline as opponent Elo rises this tournament (SOT: 7,7,4,5,2 against opponents ranked 1564,1653,1902,1872,2064 respectively -- their weakest output came against their strongest opponent by a wide margin). Argentina (2230) is stronger than every team Switzerland has faced. Built an explicit regime-adjusted estimate using only the 2 toughest-opponent games (Algeria, Colombia) as the primary reference class instead of the full 5-game average -- this is the exact check that was skipped for Belgium yesterday. Even after this adjustment, both count-props still ran meaningfully above market, so market was weighted more heavily still in the final blend (see market_gap_log). This is the substantive difference from yesterday: the check was run, it changed the number materially (naive 0.84/0.71 -> regime-adjusted 0.76/0.61 -> final blended-to-market 0.62/0.45), not just checked-and-dismissed.
symmetric_regime_coverage_check.argentina_own_props_check: Mirror direction: is Switzerland's defense good enough to suppress Argentina's own SOT/corners props? Evidence is mixed but real -- Colombia (the closest-caliber opponent Switzerland has faced) still managed 15 shots and 7 corners against Switzerland's defense, only their SOT conversion was suppressed (3 SOT from 15 shots). This is a genuine defensive discipline signal, not a total shutdown. Argentina's own count-prop models already agree closely with market (see below), suggesting the market has already priced in a reasonable amount of Swiss defensive resistance without needing further manual shading.

cards_model.note: Cards threshold and penalty-or-red both use Pinheiro's n=2 sample -- thinner than every referee sample used so far this project (Oliver n=3, Turpin n=2 but with a cleaner corroboration). One of his 2 known matches included a red card, which is doing real work in the red-card-rate shrinkage; treated as a weak but not ignorable signal.

messi_goalscorer_model.2026_empirical_rate: 5/5 games, scored in literally every match, 8 goals total -- more extreme than Haaland's 4/4 (with 1 DNP)
messi_goalscorer_model.note: Same tension as Haaland in the Norway-England session. Leaned above market given the genuinely unprecedented consistency (not a single blank all tournament) but nowhere near the raw empirical rate -- RULE6 discipline applies regardless of how hot the streak is. Final: 0.50.

sub_scorer_and_second_half_reuse.source: reused corpus base rate from Norway-England session (n=60), no match-specific adjustment
